焼津神社 | image = Yamato Takeru at Yaizu Jinja cr.jpg | caption = Statue of Yamato Takeru at Yaizu Jinja | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|51|54|N|138|18|49|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Yamato Takeru no Mikoto and 3 followers | founded_by = | established = c.409 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 2-7-1 Yaizu-chō, Yaizu, Shizuoka, 425-0026 | website = {{Official website|1=https://yaizujinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = ''[[Nagare-zukuri]]'' | festival = August 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yaizu Jinja'''|焼津神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[:en:Yaizu, Shizuoka|Yaizu]] in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine is also referred to as the {{nihongo|Irie Daimyojin|入江大明神|}}.The main festival of the shrine is annually on August 13. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Yaizu is the {{nihongo|[[Yamato Takeru]]|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, the legendary 4th century AD prince of the [[:en:Yamato dynasty|Yamato dynasty]], son of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]], whose legends are detailed in the Japanese chronicles ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Per one of the legends in the Yamato Takeru cycle, Emperor Keikō sent him to the eastern provinces, whose ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' had rebelled against the imperial court. His enemies attempted to kill him by setting fire to a grassy plain, but he escaped by using the powers of his magical sword, the ''[[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi no tsurugi]]'' and two magic stones. The sword, one of the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Imperial Regalia of Japan]] is kept at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in [[:en:Nagoya|Nagoya]], along with one of the magic stones. Yaizu Jinja claims to have the remaining stone, as well as to have been built on the location of Yamato Takeru's miraculous escape. The remaining three ''kami'' enshrined at Yaizu Jinja are the three main retainers of Yamato Takeru during his voyage east, including his brother-in-law {{nihongo|Kibi Takehiko no Mikoto|吉備武彦命|}}, {{nihongo|Otomo no Takehi no Mikoto|大伴武日連命|}} and {{nihongo|Nanatsukahagi no Mikoto|七束脛命|}} Yaizu Jinja also has numerous small subsidiary shrines dedicated to various ''kami'' within its precincts. ==History== The date of Yaizu Jinja's foundation is unknown. Per shrine tradition it was founded in 409 AD. In the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, the shrine is mentioned as one of four shrines in Mashizu District of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]. The shrine was patronized by the [[Imagawa clan]] in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], and given revenues of 500 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' for its upkeep. This was reduced to a 70 ''koku'' stipend in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1883, Yaizu Jinja was recognized as a “Prefectural Shrine” under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] within the [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] system. == Festivals == * ''Aramatsuri'' (annually on August 12–13) is the shrine's main festival. It involves a procession with two ''[[mikoshi]]'' and is considered one of the roughest festivals in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]]. ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== * Shizuokaken Kyōdokenkyūkai, ''Shizuokaken Jinja Shi'', 1980 {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Yaizu-jinja|Yaizu Jinja}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] }} [[file:Yaizu-jinja haiden.JPG|thumb|right|270px|''Haiden'' of Yaizu Jinja]] [[sn:Yaizu Shrine]] [[en:Yaizu Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Yaizu-jinja}} [[fr:Yaizu-jinja]] [[ja:焼津神社]]

焼津神社 | image = Yamato Takeru at Yaizu Jinja cr.jpg | caption = Statue of Yamato Takeru at Yaizu Jinja | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|51|54|N|138|18|49|E|region:JP_scale:30000_source:jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = Yamato Takeru no Mikoto and 3 followers | founded_by = | established = c.409 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 2-7-1 Yaizu-chō, Yaizu, Shizuoka, 425-0026 | website = {{Official website|1=https://yaizujinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = ''[[Nagare-zukuri]]'' | festival = August 13 | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Yaizu Jinja'''|焼津神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[:en:Yaizu, Shizuoka|Yaizu]] in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine is also referred to as the {{nihongo|Irie Daimyojin|入江大明神|}}.The main festival of the shrine is annually on August 13. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Yaizu is the {{nihongo|[[Yamato Takeru]]|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, the legendary 4th century AD prince of the [[:en:Yamato dynasty|Yamato dynasty]], son of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]], whose legends are detailed in the Japanese chronicles ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Per one of the legends in the Yamato Takeru cycle, Emperor Keikō sent him to the eastern provinces, whose ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' had rebelled against the imperial court. His enemies attempted to kill him by setting fire to a grassy plain, but he escaped by using the powers of his magical sword, the ''[[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi no tsurugi]]'' and two magic stones. The sword, one of the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Imperial Regalia of Japan]] is kept at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in [[:en:Nagoya|Nagoya]], along with one of the magic stones. Yaizu Jinja claims to have the remaining stone, as well as to have been built on the location of Yamato Takeru's miraculous escape. The remaining three ''kami'' enshrined at Yaizu Jinja are the three main retainers of Yamato Takeru during his voyage east, including his brother-in-law {{nihongo|Kibi Takehiko no Mikoto|吉備武彦命|}}, {{nihongo|Otomo no Takehi no Mikoto|大伴武日連命|}} and {{nihongo|Nanatsukahagi no Mikoto|七束脛命|}} Yaizu Jinja also has numerous small subsidiary shrines dedicated to various ''kami'' within its precincts. ==History== The date of Yaizu Jinja's foundation is unknown. Per shrine tradition it was founded in 409 AD. In the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, the shrine is mentioned as one of four shrines in Mashizu District of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]. The shrine was patronized by the [[Imagawa clan]] in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], and given revenues of 500 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' for its upkeep. This was reduced to a 70 ''koku'' stipend in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1883, Yaizu Jinja was recognized as a “Prefectural Shrine” under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] within the [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] system. == Festivals == * ''Aramatsuri'' (annually on August 12–13) is the shrine's main festival. It involves a procession with two ''[[mikoshi]]'' and is considered one of the roughest festivals in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]]. ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== * Shizuokaken Kyōdokenkyūkai, ''Shizuokaken Jinja Shi'', 1980 {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Yaizu-jinja|Yaizu Jinja}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] }} [[file:Yaizu-jinja haiden.JPG|thumb|right|270px|''Haiden'' of Yaizu Jinja]] [[sn:Yaizu Shrine]] [[en:Yaizu Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Yaizu-jinja}} [[fr:Yaizu-jinja]] [[ja:焼津神社]]

It involves a procession with two ''[[mikoshi]]'' and is considered one of the roughest festivals in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]]. ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== * Shizuokaken Kyōdokenkyūkai, ''Shizuokaken Jinja Shi'', 1980 {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Yaizu-jinja|Yaizu Jinja}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] }} [[file:Yaizu-jinja haiden.JPG|thumb|right|270px|''Haiden'' of Yaizu Jinja]] [[en:Yaizu Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Yaizu-jinja}} [[fr:Yaizu-jinja]] [[ja:焼津神社]] {{wikidata link|Q3571342}}

[[sn:commons:Category:Yaizu-jinja]][[en:Yaizu Shrine]][[fr:Yaizu-jinja]][[ja:焼津神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3571342]] Wikidata name: Yaizu Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yaizu Jinja
焼津神社 | image = File:Yaizu-jinja haiden.JPG | caption = Statue of Yamato Takeru at Yaizu Jinja | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.865|N|138.31361111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} | founded_by = | established = c.409 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 2-7-1 Yaizu-chō, Yaizu, Shizuoka, 425-0026 | website = {{Official website|1=https://yaizujinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} | festival = August 13 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Yaizu|WD=Q696783}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yaizu Jinja'''|焼津神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[:en:Yaizu, Shizuoka|Yaizu]] in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine is also referred to as the {{nihongo|Irie Daimyojin|入江大明神|}}.The main festival of the shrine is annually on August 13. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Yaizu is the {{nihongo|[[Yamato Takeru]]|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, the legendary 4th century AD prince of the [[:en:Yamato dynasty|Yamato dynasty]], son of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]], whose legends are detailed in the Japanese chronicles ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Per one of the legends in the Yamato Takeru cycle, Emperor Keikō sent him to the eastern provinces, whose ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' had rebelled against the imperial court. His enemies attempted to kill him by setting fire to a grassy plain, but he escaped by using the powers of his magical sword, the ''[[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi no tsurugi]]'' and two magic stones. The sword, one of the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Imperial Regalia of Japan]] is kept at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in [[:en:Nagoya|Nagoya]], along with one of the magic stones. Yaizu Jinja claims to have the remaining stone, as well as to have been built on the location of Yamato Takeru's miraculous escape. The remaining three ''kami'' enshrined at Yaizu Jinja are the three main retainers of Yamato Takeru during his voyage east, including his brother-in-law {{nihongo|Kibi Takehiko no Mikoto|吉備武彦命|}}, {{nihongo|Otomo no Takehi no Mikoto|大伴武日連命|}} and {{nihongo|Nanatsukahagi no Mikoto|七束脛命|}} Yaizu Jinja also has numerous small subsidiary shrines dedicated to various ''kami'' within its precincts. ==History== The date of Yaizu Jinja's foundation is unknown. Per shrine tradition it was founded in 409 AD. In the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, the shrine is mentioned as one of four shrines in Mashizu District of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]. The shrine was patronized by the [[Imagawa clan]] in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], and given revenues of 500 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' for its upkeep. This was reduced to a 70 ''koku'' stipend in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1883, Yaizu Jinja was recognized as a “Prefectural Shrine” under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] within the [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] system. == Festivals == * ''Aramatsuri'' (annually on August 12–13) is the shrine's main festival. It involves a procession with two ''[[mikoshi]]'' and is considered one of the roughest festivals in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]]. ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== * Shizuokaken Kyōdokenkyūkai, ''Shizuokaken Jinja Shi'', 1980 {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Yaizu-jinja|Yaizu Jinja}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] }} [[file:Yaizu-jinja haiden.JPG|thumb|right|270px|''Haiden'' of Yaizu Jinja]] [[en:Yaizu Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Yaizu-jinja}} [[fr:Yaizu-jinja]] [[ja:焼津神社]] {{wikidata link|Q3571342}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

It involves a procession with two ''[[mikoshi]]'' and is considered one of the roughest festivals in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]]. ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== * Shizuokaken Kyōdokenkyūkai, ''Shizuokaken Jinja Shi'', 1980 {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Yaizu-jinja|Yaizu Jinja}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] }} [[file:Yaizu-jinja haiden.JPG|thumb|right|270px|''Haiden'' of Yaizu Jinja]] [[en:Yaizu Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Yaizu-jinja}} [[fr:Yaizu-jinja]] [[ja:焼津神社]] {{wikidata link|Q3571342}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[sn:commons:Category:Yaizu-jinja]][[en:Yaizu Shrine]][[fr:Yaizu-jinja]][[ja:焼津神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3571342]] Wikidata name: Yaizu Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yaizu Jinja
焼津神社 | image = File:Yaizu-jinja haiden.JPG | caption = Statue of Yamato Takeru at Yaizu Jinja | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.865|N|138.31361111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} | founded_by = | established = c.409 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 2-7-1 Yaizu-chō, Yaizu, Shizuoka, 425-0026 | website = {{Official website|1=https://yaizujinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} | festival = August 13 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Yaizu|WD=Q696783}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yaizu Jinja'''|焼津神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[:en:Yaizu, Shizuoka|Yaizu]] in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine is also referred to as the {{nihongo|Irie Daimyojin|入江大明神|}}.The main festival of the shrine is annually on August 13. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Yaizu is the {{nihongo|[[Yamato Takeru]]|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, the legendary 4th century AD prince of the [[:en:Yamato dynasty|Yamato dynasty]], son of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]], whose legends are detailed in the Japanese chronicles ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Per one of the legends in the Yamato Takeru cycle, Emperor Keikō sent him to the eastern provinces, whose ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' had rebelled against the imperial court. His enemies attempted to kill him by setting fire to a grassy plain, but he escaped by using the powers of his magical sword, the ''[[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi no tsurugi]]'' and two magic stones. The sword, one of the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Imperial Regalia of Japan]] is kept at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in [[:en:Nagoya|Nagoya]], along with one of the magic stones. Yaizu Jinja claims to have the remaining stone, as well as to have been built on the location of Yamato Takeru's miraculous escape. The remaining three ''kami'' enshrined at Yaizu Jinja are the three main retainers of Yamato Takeru during his voyage east, including his brother-in-law {{nihongo|Kibi Takehiko no Mikoto|吉備武彦命|}}, {{nihongo|Otomo no Takehi no Mikoto|大伴武日連命|}} and {{nihongo|Nanatsukahagi no Mikoto|七束脛命|}} Yaizu Jinja also has numerous small subsidiary shrines dedicated to various ''kami'' within its precincts. ==History== The date of Yaizu Jinja's foundation is unknown. Per shrine tradition it was founded in 409 AD. In the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, the shrine is mentioned as one of four shrines in Mashizu District of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]. The shrine was patronized by the [[Imagawa clan]] in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], and given revenues of 500 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' for its upkeep. This was reduced to a 70 ''koku'' stipend in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1883, Yaizu Jinja was recognized as a “Prefectural Shrine” under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] within the [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] system. == Festivals == * ''Aramatsuri'' (annually on August 12–13) is the shrine's main festival. It involves a procession with two ''[[mikoshi]]'' and is considered one of the roughest festivals in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]]. ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== * Shizuokaken Kyōdokenkyūkai, ''Shizuokaken Jinja Shi'', 1980 {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Yaizu-jinja|Yaizu Jinja}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] }} [[file:Yaizu-jinja haiden.JPG|thumb|right|270px|''Haiden'' of Yaizu Jinja]] [[en:Yaizu Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Yaizu-jinja}} [[fr:Yaizu-jinja]] [[ja:焼津神社]] {{wikidata link|Q3571342}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

In 1883, Yaizu Jinja was recognized as a “Prefectural Shrine” under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] within the [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] system. == Festivals == * ''Aramatsuri'' (annually on August 12–13) is the shrine's main festival. It involves a procession with two ''[[mikoshi]]'' and is considered one of the roughest festivals in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]]. ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== * Shizuokaken Kyōdokenkyūkai, ''Shizuokaken Jinja Shi'', 1980 {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Yaizu-jinja|Yaizu Jinja}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] }} [[file:Yaizu-jinja haiden.JPG|thumb|right|270px|''Haiden'' of Yaizu Jinja]] [[en:Yaizu Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Yaizu-jinja}} [[fr:Yaizu-jinja]] [[ja:焼津神社]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q3571342}}

[[commons:Category:Yaizu-jinja]][[en:Yaizu Shrine]][[fr:Yaizu-jinja]][[ja:焼津神社]] [[commons:Category:Yaizu-jinja]][[en:Yaizu Shrine]][[fr:Yaizu-jinja]][[ja:焼津神社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Yaizu-jinja]][[en:Yaizu Shrine]][[fr:Yaizu-jinja]][[ja:焼津神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q3571342]] Wikidata name: Yaizu Shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:23, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Yaizu Jinja
焼津神社 | image = File:Yaizu-jinja haiden.JPG | caption = Statue of Yamato Takeru at Yaizu Jinja | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.865|N|138.31361111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} | founded_by = | established = c.409 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 2-7-1 Yaizu-chō, Yaizu, Shizuoka, 425-0026 | website = {{Official website|1=https://yaizujinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} | festival = August 13 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Yaizu|WD=Q696783}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yaizu Jinja'''|焼津神社}} is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] in the city of [[:en:Yaizu, Shizuoka|Yaizu]] in [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The shrine is also referred to as the {{nihongo|Irie Daimyojin|入江大明神|}}.The main festival of the shrine is annually on August 13. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Yaizu is the {{nihongo|[[Yamato Takeru]]|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, the legendary 4th century AD prince of the [[:en:Yamato dynasty|Yamato dynasty]], son of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]], whose legends are detailed in the Japanese chronicles ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Per one of the legends in the Yamato Takeru cycle, Emperor Keikō sent him to the eastern provinces, whose ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' had rebelled against the imperial court. His enemies attempted to kill him by setting fire to a grassy plain, but he escaped by using the powers of his magical sword, the ''[[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi no tsurugi]]'' and two magic stones. The sword, one of the [[:en:Imperial Regalia of Japan|Imperial Regalia of Japan]] is kept at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in [[:en:Nagoya|Nagoya]], along with one of the magic stones. Yaizu Jinja claims to have the remaining stone, as well as to have been built on the location of Yamato Takeru's miraculous escape. The remaining three ''kami'' enshrined at Yaizu Jinja are the three main retainers of Yamato Takeru during his voyage east, including his brother-in-law {{nihongo|Kibi Takehiko no Mikoto|吉備武彦命|}}, {{nihongo|Otomo no Takehi no Mikoto|大伴武日連命|}} and {{nihongo|Nanatsukahagi no Mikoto|七束脛命|}} Yaizu Jinja also has numerous small subsidiary shrines dedicated to various ''kami'' within its precincts. ==History== The date of Yaizu Jinja's foundation is unknown. Per shrine tradition it was founded in 409 AD. In the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, the shrine is mentioned as one of four shrines in Mashizu District of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]. The shrine was patronized by the [[Imagawa clan]] in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], and given revenues of 500 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' for its upkeep. This was reduced to a 70 ''koku'' stipend in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1883, Yaizu Jinja was recognized as a “Prefectural Shrine” under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] within the [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] system. == Festivals == * ''Aramatsuri'' (annually on August 12–13) is the shrine's main festival. It involves a procession with two ''[[mikoshi]]'' and is considered one of the roughest festivals in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]]. ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== * Shizuokaken Kyōdokenkyūkai, ''Shizuokaken Jinja Shi'', 1980 {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Yaizu-jinja|Yaizu Jinja}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] }} [[file:Yaizu-jinja haiden.JPG|thumb|right|270px|''Haiden'' of Yaizu Jinja]] [[en:Yaizu Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Yaizu-jinja}} [[fr:Yaizu-jinja]] [[ja:焼津神社]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q3571342}}

The remaining three ''kami'' enshrined at Yaizu Jinja are the three main retainers of Yamato Takeru during his voyage east, including his brother-in-law {{nihongo|Kibi Takehiko no Mikoto|吉備武彦命|}}, {{nihongo|Otomo no Takehi no Mikoto|大伴武日連命|}} and {{nihongo|Nanatsukahagi no Mikoto|七束脛命|}} Yaizu Jinja also has numerous small subsidiary shrines dedicated to various ''kami'' within its precincts. ==History== The date of Yaizu Jinja's foundation is unknown. Per shrine tradition it was founded in 409 AD. In the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, the shrine is mentioned as one of four shrines in Mashizu District of [[:en:Suruga Province|Suruga Province]]. The shrine was patronized by the [[Imagawa clan]] in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], and given revenues of 500 ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' for its upkeep. This was reduced to a 70 ''koku'' stipend in the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]] by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1883, Yaizu Jinja was recognized as a “Prefectural Shrine” under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] within the [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] system. == Festivals == * ''Aramatsuri'' (annually on August 12–13) is the shrine's main festival. It involves a procession with two ''[[mikoshi]]'' and is considered one of the roughest festivals in the [[:en:Tōkai region|Tōkai region]]. ==See also== * [[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== * Shizuokaken Kyōdokenkyūkai, ''Shizuokaken Jinja Shi'', 1980 {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Yaizu-jinja|Yaizu Jinja}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| }} [[file:Yaizu-jinja haiden.JPG|thumb|right|270px|''Haiden'' of Yaizu Jinja]] [[en:Yaizu Shrine]] {{Commons category|Yaizu-jinja}} [[fr:Yaizu-jinja]] [[ja:焼津神社]] {{wikidata link|Q3571342}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]]

The shrine is also referred to as the {{nihongo|Irie Daimyojin|入江大明神|}}.The main festival of the shrine is annually on August 13. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Yaizu is the {{nihongo|[[Yamato Takeru]]|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, the legendary 4th century AD prince of the [[:en:Yamato dynasty|Yamato dynasty]], son of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]], whose legends are detailed in the Japanese chronicles ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. The shrine is also referred to as the {{nihongo|Irie Daimyojin|入江大明神|}}.The main festival of the shrine is annually on August 13. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Yaizu is the {{nihongo|[[Yamato Takeru]]|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, the legendary 4th century AD prince of the [[:en:Yamato dynasty|Yamato dynasty]], son of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō}}, whose legends are detailed in the Japanese chronicles ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Per one of the legends in the Yamato Takeru cycle, Emperor Keikō sent him to the eastern provinces, whose ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' had rebelled against the imperial court. His enemies attempted to kill him by setting fire to a grassy plain, but he escaped by using the powers of his magical sword, the ''[[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi no tsurugi]]'' and two magic stones. The sword, one of the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|WD=Q327465|lt=Imperial Regalia of Japan}} is kept at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in {{ill|Nagoya|en|Nagoya|WD=Q11751|lt=Nagoya}}, along with one of the magic stones. Yaizu Jinja claims to have the remaining stone, as well as to have been built on the location of Yamato Takeru's miraculous escape. The remaining three ''kami'' enshrined at Yaizu Jinja are the three main retainers of Yamato Takeru during his voyage east, including his brother-in-law {{nihongo|Kibi Takehiko no Mikoto|吉備武彦命|}}, {{nihongo|Otomo no Takehi no Mikoto|大伴武日連命|}} and {{nihongo|Nanatsukahagi no Mikoto|七束脛命|}} Yaizu Jinja also has numerous small subsidiary shrines dedicated to various ''kami'' within its precincts. ==History== The date of Yaizu Jinja's foundation is unknown. Per shrine tradition it was founded in 409 AD. In the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, the shrine is mentioned as one of four shrines in Mashizu District of {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}}. The shrine was patronized by the [[Imagawa clan]] in the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, and given revenues of 500 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'' for its upkeep. This was reduced to a 70 ''koku'' stipend in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1883, Yaizu Jinja was recognized as a “Prefectural Shrine” under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] within the {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} system. == Festivals == * ''Aramatsuri'' (annually on August 12–13) is the shrine's main festival. It involves a procession with two ''[[mikoshi]]'' and is considered one of the roughest festivals in the {{ill|Tōkai region|en|Tōkai region|WD=Q398989|lt=Tōkai region}}. ==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} ==References== * Shizuokaken Kyōdokenkyūkai, ''Shizuokaken Jinja Shi'', 1980 {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Yaizu-jinja|Yaizu Jinja}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[file:Yaizu-jinja haiden.JPG|thumb|right|270px|''Haiden'' of Yaizu Jinja]] [[en:Yaizu Shrine]] {{Commons category|Yaizu-jinja}} [[fr:Yaizu-jinja]] [[ja:焼津神社]] {{wikidata link|Q3571342}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]]

焼津神社 | image = File:Yaizu-jinja haiden.JPG | caption = Statue of Yamato Takeru at Yaizu Jinja | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.865|N|138.31361111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} | founded_by = | established = c.409 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 2-7-1 Yaizu-chō, Yaizu, Shizuoka, 425-0026 | website = {{Official website|1=https://yaizujinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} | festival = August 13 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Yaizu|WD=Q696783}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yaizu Jinja'''|焼津神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the city of [[:en:Yaizu, Shizuoka|Yaizu]] in {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The shrine is also referred to as the {{nihongo|Irie Daimyojin|入江大明神|}}.The main festival of the shrine is annually on August 13. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Yaizu is the {{nihongo|[[Yamato Takeru]]|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, the legendary 4th century AD prince of the [[:en:Yamato dynasty|Yamato dynasty]], son of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō}}, whose legends are detailed in the Japanese chronicles ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Per one of the legends in the Yamato Takeru cycle, Emperor Keikō sent him to the eastern provinces, whose ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' had rebelled against the imperial court. His enemies attempted to kill him by setting fire to a grassy plain, but he escaped by using the powers of his magical sword, the ''[[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi no tsurugi]]'' and two magic stones. The sword, one of the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|WD=Q327465|lt=Imperial Regalia of Japan}} is kept at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in {{ill|Nagoya|en|Nagoya|WD=Q11751|lt=Nagoya}}, along with one of the magic stones. Yaizu Jinja claims to have the remaining stone, as well as to have been built on the location of Yamato Takeru's miraculous escape. The remaining three ''kami'' enshrined at Yaizu Jinja are the three main retainers of Yamato Takeru during his voyage east, including his brother-in-law {{nihongo|Kibi Takehiko no Mikoto|吉備武彦命|}}, {{nihongo|Otomo no Takehi no Mikoto|大伴武日連命|}} and {{nihongo|Nanatsukahagi no Mikoto|七束脛命|}} Yaizu Jinja also has numerous small subsidiary shrines dedicated to various ''kami'' within its precincts. ==History== The date of Yaizu Jinja's foundation is unknown. Per shrine tradition it was founded in 409 AD. In the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, the shrine is mentioned as one of four shrines in Mashizu District of {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}}. The shrine was patronized by the [[Imagawa clan]] in the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, and given revenues of 500 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'' for its upkeep. This was reduced to a 70 ''koku'' stipend in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1883, Yaizu Jinja was recognized as a “Prefectural Shrine” under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] within the {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} system. == Festivals == * ''Aramatsuri'' (annually on August 12–13) is the shrine's main festival. It involves a procession with two ''[[mikoshi]]'' and is considered one of the roughest festivals in the {{ill|Tōkai region|en|Tōkai region|WD=Q398989|lt=Tōkai region}}. ==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} ==References== * Shizuokaken Kyōdokenkyūkai, ''Shizuokaken Jinja Shi'', 1980 {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Yaizu-jinja|Yaizu Jinja}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[file:Yaizu-jinja haiden.JPG|thumb|right|270px|''Haiden'' of Yaizu Jinja]] [[en:Yaizu Shrine]] {{Commons category|Yaizu-jinja}} [[fr:Yaizu-jinja]] [[ja:焼津神社]] {{wikidata link|Q3571342}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

焼津神社 | image = File:Yaizu-jinja haiden.JPG | caption = Statue of Yamato Takeru at Yaizu Jinja | map_type = Japan Shizuoka Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.865|N|138.31361111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Yamato Takeru|WD=Q461258}} | founded_by = | established = c.409 AD | date_destroyed = | location = 2-7-1 Yaizu-chō, Yaizu, Shizuoka, 425-0026 | website = {{Official website|1=https://yaizujinja.or.jp}} | architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} | festival = August 13 | leadership = | location = {{ill|Yaizu|WD=Q696783}} }} {{nihongo|'''Yaizu Jinja'''|焼津神社}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} in the city of [[:en:Yaizu, Shizuoka|Yaizu]] in {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The shrine is also referred to as the {{nihongo|Irie Daimyojin|入江大明神|}}.The main festival of the shrine is annually on August 13. ==Enshrined ''kami''== The primary ''[[kami]]'' of Yaizu is the {{nihongo|[[Yamato Takeru]]|日本武尊|1=Yamato Takeru|lt=Prince Yamato Takeru|3=ヤマトタケル}}, the legendary 4th century AD prince of the [[:en:Yamato dynasty|Yamato dynasty]], son of {{ill|Emperor Keikō|en|Emperor Keikō|WD=Q329723|lt=Emperor Keikō}}, whose legends are detailed in the Japanese chronicles ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. Per one of the legends in the Yamato Takeru cycle, Emperor Keikō sent him to the eastern provinces, whose ''[[Kuni no miyatsuko]]'' had rebelled against the imperial court. His enemies attempted to kill him by setting fire to a grassy plain, but he escaped by using the powers of his magical sword, the ''[[Kusanagi no Tsurugi|Kusanagi no tsurugi]]'' and two magic stones. The sword, one of the {{ill|Imperial Regalia of Japan|en|Imperial Regalia of Japan|WD=Q327465|lt=Imperial Regalia of Japan}} is kept at [[:en:Atsuta Jingu|Atsuta Jingu]] in {{ill|Nagoya|en|Nagoya|WD=Q11751|lt=Nagoya}}, along with one of the magic stones. Yaizu Jinja claims to have the remaining stone, as well as to have been built on the location of Yamato Takeru's miraculous escape. The remaining three ''kami'' enshrined at Yaizu Jinja are the three main retainers of Yamato Takeru during his voyage east, including his brother-in-law {{nihongo|Kibi Takehiko no Mikoto|吉備武彦命|}}, {{nihongo|Otomo no Takehi no Mikoto|大伴武日連命|}} and {{nihongo|Nanatsukahagi no Mikoto|七束脛命|}} Yaizu Jinja also has numerous small subsidiary shrines dedicated to various ''kami'' within its precincts. ==History== The date of Yaizu Jinja's foundation is unknown. Per shrine tradition it was founded in 409 AD. In the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, the shrine is mentioned as one of four shrines in Mashizu District of {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}}. The shrine was patronized by the [[Imagawa clan]] in the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, and given revenues of 500 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku}}'' for its upkeep. This was reduced to a 70 ''koku'' stipend in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}} by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1883, Yaizu Jinja was recognized as a “Prefectural Shrine” under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] within the {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto}} system. == Festivals == * ''Aramatsuri'' (annually on August 12–13) is the shrine's main festival. It involves a procession with two ''[[mikoshi]]'' and is considered one of the roughest festivals in the {{ill|Tōkai region|en|Tōkai region|WD=Q398989|lt=Tōkai region}}. ==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} ==References== * Shizuokaken Kyōdokenkyūkai, ''Shizuokaken Jinja Shi'', 1980 {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Yaizu-jinja|Yaizu Jinja}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[file:Yaizu-jinja haiden.JPG|thumb|right|270px|''Haiden'' of Yaizu Jinja]] [[en:Yaizu Shrine]] {{Commons category|Yaizu-jinja}} [[fr:Yaizu-jinja]] [[ja:焼津神社]] {{wikidata link|Q3571342}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

Yaizu Jinja claims to have the remaining stone, as well as to have been built on the location of Yamato Takeru's miraculous escape. The remaining three ''kami'' enshrined at Yaizu Jinja are the three main retainers of Yamato Takeru during his voyage east, including his brother-in-law {{nihongo|Kibi Takehiko no Mikoto|吉備武彦命|}}, {{nihongo|Otomo no Takehi no Mikoto|大伴武日連命|}} and {{nihongo|Nanatsukahagi no Mikoto|七束脛命|}} Yaizu Jinja also has numerous small subsidiary shrines dedicated to various ''kami'' within its precincts. ==History== The date of Yaizu Jinja's foundation is unknown. Per shrine tradition it was founded in 409 AD. In the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, the shrine is mentioned as one of four shrines in Mashizu District of {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province|1=Suruga Province}}. The shrine was patronized by the [[Imagawa clan]] in the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, and given revenues of 500 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku|1=Koku}}'' for its upkeep. This was reduced to a 70 ''koku'' stipend in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1883, Yaizu Jinja was recognized as a “Prefectural Shrine” under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] within the {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} system. == Festivals == * ''Aramatsuri'' (annually on August 12–13) is the shrine's main festival. It involves a procession with two ''[[mikoshi]]'' and is considered one of the roughest festivals in the {{ill|Tōkai region|en|Tōkai region|WD=Q398989|lt=Tōkai region|1=Tōkai region}}. ==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} ==References== * Shizuokaken Kyōdokenkyūkai, ''Shizuokaken Jinja Shi'', 1980 {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Yaizu-jinja|Yaizu Jinja}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[file:Yaizu-jinja haiden.JPG|thumb|right|270px|''Haiden'' of Yaizu Jinja]] [[en:Yaizu Shrine]] {{Commons category|Yaizu-jinja}} [[fr:Yaizu-jinja]] [[ja:焼津神社]] {{wikidata link|Q3571342}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

Per shrine tradition it was founded in 409 AD. In the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} ''[[Engishiki]]'' records dated 927 AD, the shrine is mentioned as one of four shrines in Mashizu District of {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province|1=Suruga Province}}. The shrine was patronized by the [[Imagawa clan]] in the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, and given revenues of 500 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku|1=Koku}}'' for its upkeep. This was reduced to a 70 ''koku'' stipend in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} by the [[Tokugawa shogunate]]. In 1883, Yaizu Jinja was recognized as a “Prefectural Shrine” under the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] within the {{ill|State Shinto|en|State Shinto|WD=Q1197248|lt=State Shinto|1=State Shinto}} system. == Festivals == * ''Aramatsuri'' (annually on August 12–13) is the shrine's main festival. It involves a procession with two ''[[mikoshi]]'' and is considered one of the roughest festivals in the {{ill|Tōkai region|en|Tōkai region|WD=Q398989|lt=Tōkai region|1=Tōkai region}}. ==See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} ==References== * Shizuokaken Kyōdokenkyūkai, ''Shizuokaken Jinja Shi'', 1980 {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category-inline|Yaizu-jinja|Yaizu Jinja}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[file:Yaizu-jinja haiden.JPG|thumb|right|270px|''Haiden'' of Yaizu Jinja]] {{Commons category|Yaizu-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q3571342|co|commons:Category:Yaizu-jinja|en|Yaizu Shrine|fr|Yaizu-jinja|ja|焼津神社}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]]
